speaker: interviewer
text: You have all your hair.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: It must be in the genes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- no. That's because I always have a kippah on. That protects my hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, no. When I was in the Army, I had hair. When I came out of the Army, I lost all my hair, because I had a big kippah on my head.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's nothing--

speaker: interviewer
text: They called that a helmet. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It's nothing to explain. I just kippah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You keep on the kippah.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Keeps the sun out of the scalp. No, it's still in the genes. My genes have no hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's talk more.

speaker: interviewee
text: I should?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure, say a few words.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, you see, I tried to groom my hair, and comb them, and let them grow the way it is, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but it grows beautifully.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you very much. I appreciate it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I like the color. It comes out of a bottle or it comes out natural? Today is June 7th, 1995. The survivor is Cantor Morris Lang. The interviewer's Chaim Rosov. We're in Coconut Creek, Florida in the United States. The language that will be spoken is English. And this, as Gera wants me to say, is tape number 1. They eliminate this. It's not part of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Today is June 7th, 1995. I'm interviewing Cantor Morris Lang. I am Chaim Rosov. We are in Coconut Creek, Florida and the language that we will be speaking and the interview we'll be taking is in English. Good afternoon, Cantor Lang. I'd like to get started by you telling me when you were born and where you were born. I was born on June the 1st, 1921. I was born in Krasnik, which is a little town, population 12,000, half of it were Jewish people. And the town Krasnik is not far from Lublin.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me about your family, about your parents, your sisters and brothers, your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I come from my family which we were eight children at home. My father and mother were very devoted to Jews. We had a upbringing as we could call it today in the Orthodox traditional way. We had a warm home, a loving home, and we knew each other very well. And I thought that in those days before the war, we were not well-- not too well [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] off, but we had--

speaker: interviewer
text: Comfortable.

speaker: interviewee
text: Comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest brother was Chaim Yisroel. He got married a few years before the war, and he lived in a town-- a village called [POLISH], which was about 25 kilometers away from Krasnik. He had a lovely wife. And when the war broke out, he had already two children. A younger brother after him was named Sholom, which I will tell you about him later. Then I had a sister, Golda. We called her Goldala. And she married just-- just around before the world war broke out, and she lived in Belzyce. Belzyce was a town also in the [POLISH], but it was on the other side of Krasnik. And her a husband's name was Shmiel, Shmiel Gradel. Her name was Gradel, and she lived there in-- in I would say, a reasonably comfortable-- comfortable way. Then I came. I was the fourth child. Then after me, I had a sister by the name of Chavahla. Then at two years younger than her was her sister, Nechala. Then two years younger than her, I had a brother. We called him Yehshua or Shuale. And the youngest sister of mine was named was named Rosala. Rosala must have been about seven or eight years old when the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of a school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went, first of all, to elementary school or as we call it here, public school. I finished public school. It was the equivalent here to high school. I had to go eight-- I don't remember-- eight or 10 grades, which was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was a-- quite a liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in school at the time, did you find any anti-Semitism in the school or the teachers--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or the students?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. There were times that we were only four or five Jews in that class, and we were constantly harassed. But I was a young guy. I didn't let myself. I taught them back a lesson whenever I could. But we were harassed in school and we felt anti-Semitism. You see, the school was open on Saturday, too. And the Jewish students did not attend on a Saturday. When we came back Monday to school, we had to make up the work for Saturday day, too. But somehow we never falled behind. All of us were good students.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the teachers treat you there? Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: The teachers treat us nice, because to them they had to show impartiality. But they liked us, because we were good students.

speaker: interviewer
text: That makes sense. OK. How did-- how did the world that you lived in change when the Germans came? What changes took place?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- I would say, everything almost in Jewish life was cut off. When the war broke out in-- I think it was September 1st, 1939--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right away-- three days later, we were bombarded. Because in the town where I was born was the 24th Division of Cavalry, the 24 Pulk Ulanow, they called it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the Polish division.

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish. Therefore, they didn't hit the caserna. They didn't hit where the soldiers were, but mostly bombs fell in the Jewish quarters. And right away we encountered many, many--

speaker: interviewer
text: Deaths.

speaker: interviewee
text: Deaths. People fell from that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this done on purpose, do you think, or by accident?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know whether it was on purpose or accident. I know we were affected by it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your family directly affected by it? What happened to your parents, and your brothers, and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- fortunately, the bombs fell only about a few blocks away where we lived. And we didn't-- we wasn't-- we weren't affected. So we lived in a house where the entire family-- like my grandmother, my uncles, my cousins, and aunts-- we all lived in the same house. It was a family house. So we thanked God that we weren't affected by it. But right away, a few days later, it started up. We couldn't get to purchase food. Everything got closed up, especially Jewish stores and whatever there was. But somehow, we-- we tried to live what we had stored up and the things what we could bring in until the Germans came in about two weeks later. And when they came in and occupied Krasnik, the town, we were taken out of the houses every day. Right away, they tried to organize a Judenrat. And I remember the day when they came in, they took out the eldest. And mostly they took out the [NON-ENGLISH] rabbi and his son, also a rabbi. And they took out the eldest from the [POLISH], or as we could say, from the Jewish council, the members. And they put them up in the market. And they said, they will shot them to death if they ever wouldn't-- if we wouldn't bring out all the ammunition and arms into there. And you know, Jewish people didn't have any revolvers or arms to bring out. And we didn't. So they demanded a certain amount of real coffee. And of course, it was given to them. And a whole day, they was threatening them to shoot, and they let them go. Because I think the-- the amount of coffee they received, they were big coffee drinkers. So that saved them, and everybody went back to their homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then it started a daily struggle. First of all, there was a shortage of food. A while later, they gave out food cards, or stamps, or coupons. And with this we could buy only under their supervision, we could buy only a certain amount of food. And of course, kosher meat was not available. And it was a danger to bring it in, because we were watched, and a struggle. And once, they caught a kosher butcher selling meat quietly and secretly, they took him out in the front of everybody and they shot him dead. And the struggles began. We had to go to work. And sometimes, I as a young-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a young man of 18 years old, were glad to go out and work for them to maybe I could be able to organize something to bring home and to my family. Sometimes I was successful in organizing an extra piece of bread or a few potatoes. And sometimes it didn't work. I came home and-- well, we managed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this-- this lasted until when?

speaker: interviewee
text: This last lasted until I was awaken from sleep once in the fall or in the spring of 1940. And I was taken away on an Army truck with a few-- with a bunch of others, Jews. And they took us away about 100, 150 miles or kilometers away from Krasnik And to a place called-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh, I forgot the name what it was called. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Would you remember the name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Ruda--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was near Savin, not far from Chelm-- Ruda Opalin.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was going to say Ruda--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ruda. Ruda Opalin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was there and interrogated into this camp for a whole summer and including the winter until about January 1941, where took sick. And they liquidated the camp around December. Because our work in this camp was to regulate a river and to build--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- a dam?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dam. And we kept on doing. They didn't give us too much food there, but it was still better then I found out later on when I was taken to concentration camps. But it was very unhealthy. The sanitary--

speaker: interviewer
text: Conditions, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Conditions were there awful. We have-- we had-- we were full of lice. And when I remember those times, I used to joke around later that the only little-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] little of-- of-- of-- comfort that I got is when I was itching and I could scratch myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Then you moved on to Budzyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, not yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then not yet. Then I was throughout the end of '41. And then in 1942, in the beginning of 1942, I kept on-- had a steady place of work for-- I worked for the Kommandantur. And I had already a certificate that I'm working for them. And of course, in March of 1942, we received a message through a Polack that my oldest brother, Chaim Yisroel, was taken out that night from his home. His wife and two children were left, and he was taken to Majdanek. Of course, we didn't know where he was taken. But later on, I found out that he was taken to Majdanek. And of course, the Passover approached. And it was very hard, and we managed to scramble through to have some kind of a few matzahs in the house. And I remembered that the Seder of my-- with my parents when I saw them the last time and the rest of my sisters and brother that we were together was on Passover, 1942. And then three days later, and to be exact, on April the 12th-- I'll never forget this date, 1942. It was a Sunday, three days after Pesach. Around 12 o'clock, the entire city was circled around with police, [NON-ENGLISH], SS soldiers, and Polish police, too-- with dogs and with loud speakers. And they called upon all the Jewish people must leave their homes. They told us that we must be resettled [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and because it's a law of the German government. So therefore, take along your most precious possessions and come out to the marketplace. And whoever will disobey this law will be immediately shot. And this they go around with speakers. And you're debating whether you should go out from the house or not. There comes another order. We will come into the houses. Whoever will found in the house, somebody will be immediately shot. Take along your most precious possessions and come out. And as we looked out through the window, there were a few already people shot that ran, that weren't in their homes, or were at a neighbor's house. If they got across the street, they shot them right away. And of course, this spread a lot of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Fear.

speaker: interviewee
text: Terror and fear among us. So what do you think my parents grabbed? My father took his prayer shawl and tefillin. My mother took her little two candlesticks and candles that wherever we come, at least we should be able to observe-- Shabbos.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Shabbos and first mitzvah, mitzvot that were-- [INAUDIBLE]. And I don't remember that have we have any bread at that time, because we had left a few pieces of matzah, and they took along with them. And we got into the marketplace. Shall I explain to you what happened? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: When were get in the marketplace, I came out and I showed them my certificate that I worked the Commander, for the German Commander, so they let me out. And I thought that I'll be able to pull along a few members of my family. And I was utterly shut off. On the other side, I couldn't take anybody. And that was the last time I saw my parents and my sisters and brother. They were all put in in five in abreast or six in abreast and marched to the Bahnhof station, which was the railroad station.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as they marched with them, by the time they marched with them, already [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] day-- night fall took place. They took them, and we didn't know happened. But all we heard is shooting, and yelling, and screaming, and crying from the people. Well, I went back to my home, and I was staying in there. And I looked around, and I don't have anybody. I was alone left. Then I heard that some of the Judenrat members came and said, whoever could go out, please help us to gather. There's a lot of dead people. Let us gather them and bring them to a burial place. And of course, I went out that night with a few others. We gathered all the dead people. And if I recall, there must have been 80, 85 people-- women, children. And we brought-- we took them a place called the morgue. And I went and looked at everyone's face, and I couldn't recognize anybody that should be from my family. I lifted up my eyes to God and I thanked him that at least I didn't see anybody dead of my family. Little did I know at that time, that would have been a blessing if I could get somebody from the my-- from my family that I could at least bring them to a Jewish burial and the cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, to find out later what happened is they took them in to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to train, to wagons, or to car cattle's. And they just put them in there. 100, 150 in a car cattle, as many as they could. And of course, by the night end, they took them away to a non-disclosed place. We-- it took us a long time to find out from the Polacks that they took the transport to Belzec, which was not far from-- it was also, I think, in the Lublin [POLISH],

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, the area.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's in the Lublin District. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I didn't have an idea what the Belzec place is. Of course, they told us that it's a camp. Only to find out later that this was the first of places that the German SS has organized to bring people there and to finish them off in the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't take too long. I was called in for about a month. I had to go to work to the Germans. And then one day, I was told I have to get into a group of people, and they took us to a park. They called it P Park, near the Bahnhof station or railroad station. And we were already-- we couldn't leave that place anymore. And we stood there for about two months. And while they were-- while they-- it happened while we were there is that they-- and closer to when in October or the high holidays-- they had another [GERMAN], which means Krasnik had to become judenrein, free of Jews. In the meanwhile, the Judenrat has organized a little camp in Krasnik, and there were left a few people there that worked. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some-- some workers that could produce like shoe makers, and tailors, and you know, craftsmanship-- a few. And they needed them to do something. And I think that some of the SS had created this little camp to-- to be able to say that they are employed, that they have a camp, not to take them to the front of whatever it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happens from here?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us to Budzyn right after this. And we know that there is no more Jewish people and they're free. In the meanwhile, while they took us-- while we were at this camp in park near the railroad station, I found out that my elder brother, Sholom, which married in the beginning of the war, and he lived in a little town, [INAUDIBLE], which was also in the Lublin District. That it happened there. That they took out all the Jews there, and he ran away from there by foot, and he came into Krasnik. And he find out from whoever he asked that I was interrogated in this park. And I was able to get him in. And then I was with my brother together. Then after a while, they took us out all from there. And they took us by foot, which was 6 kilometers away to Budzyn camp. And in Budzyn, I was there until the end of '43, till about-- what, for about two years. The camp was called a labor camp not a concentration camps in the beginning. Then we had our daily portion of a little-- a little piece of bread, and soup, and so-called coffee, which was only [NON-ENGLISH], it was black. And we had to live in it. And we had to work. They created there a [GERMAN] that they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they worked out there were two camps. One in one side and one across the street, which the Poles helped have builded up an ammunition [NON-ENGLISH]. So they took it over, and they worked on parts for airplanes there. And after 1942, at one time-- or it was in the beginning of '43-- some of-- of us tried to organize to escape from there knowing that there was no way to escape. But they started to dig a tunnel. And somehow by coincidence, they caught. And in one day, they shot about 75 or 80 people in that camp. And of course, that brought again a great fear and terror to the rest of us that were left. And you know how it felt, people you know that they were shot. And they were the mostly shot around the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they built a-- a toilet, you know. That took 40, 50 people could go in at one time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in that toilet, most of the people there were shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the toilet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- you know, every time, my brother and I, we thank God that we weren't among them. And we kept on going just to sustain ourselves to be able to live through hoping that the time will come when we will be freed from there. And at the end-- also at one time, at the beginning in 1943 in June-- in January or February, they took us out from that camp because they found out it was too much in the woods. And therefore, there were some partisans

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --happenings. And they took us out, and put us in striped clothes, and declared that we are now no more in a working camp, but in a konzentrationslager, a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's when they gave you the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then at that time, everybody was given--

speaker: interviewer
text: Two letters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two letters, a K and an L, which means konzentrationslager. But this came off through the years that I wore a watch, they at that time, they didn't have any watches, I mean, from the perspiration, it got off.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't do a good job.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, so we were there. We were there working. And one time in 1943, I don't remember the time, I took sick on a typhus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Remember I told you about this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were about 28 men in a special room called a lazaret or hospital. And one morning, while my brother went to work, they took us out. And took us to a place where we had to undress. And they-- five in a row, with machine guns, they shot them. And I could see in the front of me, dead bodies overturned into that pit. And I was in the last row. I said-- I thought to myself--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's over.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if I did it consciously or unconsciously, I don't have to rush. And of course, I started in my on way to chant the Viduy, the prayer for the dead. Then when it came out, I took off my jacket, and I had to almost half undress. I said to the kommandant, herr kommandant, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. That means that I'm a singer and I could entertain him if he lets me live. And he said, yeah? Sing for me. And I-- I almost-- I didn't know what to do, so I started to chant. And I started to say, [SINGING] [HEBREW] And I choked. And he said to the Jewish kommandant, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. So he-- and I knew at the time that I'm in danger. Because anyone who possessed a little qualification or-- you know what I mean-- they destroyed. Because this kommandant, his name was Feiks. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I don't remember. I mean, he couldn't go and eat breakfast until he shoot two, three Jews in the camp just for him to have an appetite for breakfast. That's what he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Feiks. [GERMAN] Feiks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: F-Y-K-E-S or he spelled with an I. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. OK.
